narrative: Dear Alex, I know this rejection letter stings—dreaming of law school only to have your application turned away because of a prediction about the bar exam feels deeply unfair, and I'm here to walk you through why the system saw things the way it did, like sharing the story behind a tough decision from a wise mentor. Picture your profile coming together like chapters in a book: your Year 1 grades landing you in the 2nd decile stood out as the strongest signal, painting a picture of an early struggle to keep pace with top performers right from the start. That set the tone, and then your Year 3 decile of 3 reinforced it, showing the gap hadn't fully closed even after time to adjust. Your LSAT score of 35 added weight to that narrative, suggesting the kind of analytical sharpness the bar demands might be a stretch based on patterns from others like you. Coming from an upper-middle family income, while a solid foundation, didn't shift the story toward success here—it actually nudged things the other way, as the model sees students in your bracket facing unique pressures that sometimes lead to bar stumbles. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.0 fit into this too, like another thread of steady but not standout academic drive. Being a white male pursuing full-time studies wrapped in demographics that, in this model's view, align more often with failure risks than triumphs. Every piece—your race, gender, and full-time path—pulled in the same direction, creating a 57.9% chance of not passing the bar that tipped the scales against admission. It's not about your worth or potential; it's this specific mosaic of your journey so far that echoed too many past stories of tough bar outcomes. The good news? You're early in the game—boosting those deciles or LSAT could rewrite your story entirely for the next round. You've got the grit to turn this around, Alex; this is just one chapter, not the ending. Keep pushing forward.
